FT_IC1.evidence: Baseline data from U.S. ABCD Study, 21 sites, ages 9-10 years, N=10,712

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using latent profile analysis and multilevel logistic regression on ABCD baseline data

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time examined as component of composite "movement behaviors" outcome (physical activity, screen time, sleep), not isolated as standalone SM/phone IV paired with individual-trait outcome. Family environment variables (parental acceptance, monitoring, conflict) are contextual/family-level factors, not individual-trait-level variables. Paper focuses on family typologies predicting movement behavior adherence, not SM/phone use predicting individual outcomes.

notes: Screen time is included in outcome composite but not as independent phone/SM exposure variable. Exclusion code: FT-EC2

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study baseline data, U.S., ages 9-11 years, N=9,718 school-age children

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using machine learning framework to identify factors associated with fluid cognitive functioning

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Child Development

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity identified post-hoc as one of 47 predictors in composite ML model with no separately-reported association with FCF. While screening media activity is mentioned as "novel modifiable contributor" and appears in variable importance ranking, no specific quantitative result (β, OR, effect size) isolates the SM/phone-to-FCF relationship. ML feature importance rankings alone do not constitute reported numeric estimates of association for individual variables.

notes: Screen media activity appears as feature importance rank but no specific quantitative coefficient/aOR/result reported for SM-FCF association. Exclusion code: FT-EC2

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study baseline and one-year follow-up, U.S., ages 9-10 at baseline, N=11,025

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis with logistic regression examining associations between baseline screen time and binge-eating disorder at follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in International Journal of Eating Disorders

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time modalities isolated by type as IVs: social networking (aOR 1.62), texting (aOR 1.40), TV/movies (aOR 1.39), videos, video games, video chat. Binge-eating disorder is individual mental health outcome. Specific SM/phone modalities paired with individual-trait-level psychiatric/behavioral outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: each additional hour total screen time aOR 1.11 (95% CI 1.05-1.18); social networking aOR 1.62 (95% CI 1.18-2.22); texting aOR 1.40 (95% CI 1.08-1.82); TV/movies aOR 1.39 (95% CI 1.14-1.69)

FT_IC4.evidence: Adjusted odds ratios (aOR) with 95% confidence intervals reported for SM variables in logistic regression models adjusting for covariates

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design (baseline to 1-year follow-up) but screen time is IV in all analyses; binge-eating disorder is DV. Screen time is not in DV-only role, so EC7 exclusion does not apply.
notes: Paper meets all inclusion criteria. Prospective associations between specific screen modalities and individual psychiatric outcome.
